subject: maths
title: Sequences and Series
book: NCERT Class 11 maths — Sequences and Series

nodes.title: Define a sequence as an ordered list given by a rule
nodes.brief: Recognise a sequence as an ordered arrangement of numbers (its terms) generated by a rule, and view it as a function on the natural numbers.
nodes.quote: a sequence can be regarded as a function whose domain is the set of natural numbers or some subset of it.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What a sequence is.
A sequence is a collection of numbers listed in a definite order; each number in the list is called a term, written a_1, a_2, a_3, ..., a_n, where the subscript marks the position. Example: the sequence of even numbers has a_n = 2n, giving 2, 4, 6, 8, ....

PART 2 — How terms are generated.
The rule generating the terms can be an explicit formula (a_n = 2n-1 for odd numbers), a recurrence (Fibonacci: a_1 = a_2 = 1, a_n = a_{n-2} + a_{n-1}), or a verbal description (the primes). For the recurrence a_1 = 1, a_n = a_{n-1} + 3, the terms are 1, 4, 7, 10, 13.

PART 3 — Sequence as a function.
Most usefully, a sequence is a function whose domain is the set of natural numbers: a_n is simply the value of that function at n. The notation f(n) = a_n makes this concrete — every subscript n is just an input, and a_n is the output.
keyMoves: Write the terms of a sequence as a_1, a_2, a_3, ..., a_n with the subscript marking position
Generate terms from a given rule, whether a formula, a recurrence, or a verbal description
View a sequence as a function whose domain is the natural numbers (or a subset)
misconceptions: Thinking the terms of a sequence need not be in any particular order
Believing every sequence must come from a single algebraic formula

nodes.title: Classify a sequence as finite or infinite
nodes.brief: Decide whether a sequence is finite (a fixed number of terms) or infinite (it never ends).
nodes.quote: A sequence containing finite number of terms is called a *finite sequence*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Finite sequences.
A sequence containing a fixed, limited number of terms is called a finite sequence. Example: the sequence 2, 4, 8, 16, ..., 1024 has exactly 10 terms (powers of 2 from 2^1 to 2^10), so it is finite.

PART 2 — Infinite sequences.
A sequence is infinite if it never ends — it goes on without stopping. Example: the successive decimal approximations to 10/3 give 3, 3.3, 3.33, 3.333, ..., which is infinite because there is always one more term.

PART 3 — How to classify.
Count (or determine) whether the sequence has a last term. A sequence written as a_1, a_2, ..., a_n is finite; one written as a_1, a_2, a_3, ... without a stated final term is infinite. Note: '...' alone does not decide — the context of a stated last term makes it finite.
keyMoves: Count whether the sequence has a fixed (finite) number of terms
Call it a finite sequence if the number of terms is fixed
Call it an infinite sequence if it never ends
misconceptions: Thinking a sequence with large but fixed length is infinite
Believing a sequence written with '...' must always be infinite, even when it terminates at a stated last term

nodes.title: Find the nth (general) term of a sequence
nodes.brief: Read off or substitute into the formula for a_n to obtain any required term of a sequence.
nodes.quote: The *n*<sup>th</sup> term is the number at the *n*<sup>th</sup> position of the sequence and is denoted by  $a_n$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What the general term means.
The nth term a_n is the number sitting at position n in the sequence; it is called the general term because knowing a_n as a formula in n lets you produce every term by substitution. Example: if a_n = 2n + 5 then a_1 = 7, a_2 = 9, a_3 = 11.

PART 2 — Evaluating a formula-based general term.
Substitute the required n directly into the formula. For a_n = (n-1)(2-n)(3+n), find a_4: (4-1)(2-4)(3+4) = 3 * (-2) * 7 = -42. No recurrence needed — just plug in.

PART 3 — Evaluating a recurrence-based general term.
When the rule is a recurrence such as a_1 = 1, a_n = a_{n-1} + 2, build the terms one at a time: a_1 = 1, a_2 = 3, a_3 = 5, a_4 = 7, a_5 = 9. There is no shortcut — go step by step up to the required position.
keyMoves: Substitute the required value of n into the formula a_n to get that term
For a recurrence, compute the terms one after another up to the one needed
Identify a_n as the general term that produces every member of the sequence
misconceptions: Confusing the position index n with the value of the term a_n
Forgetting to apply a recurrence step by step and trying to jump straight to a_n

nodes.title: Form the series of a sequence and write it in sigma notation
nodes.brief: Build the series a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ... from a sequence and write it compactly with the summation symbol.
nodes.quote: is called the *series associated with the given sequence*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What a series is.
Given a sequence a_1, a_2, ..., a_n, the expression a_1 + a_2 + ... + a_n is called the series associated with that sequence. A series is finite or infinite according as the sequence is finite or infinite. Example: from the sequence 1, 3, 5, 7 the associated series is 1 + 3 + 5 + 7, and its sum is 16.

PART 2 — Sigma notation.
A series is written compactly using sigma (sum) notation: sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k means add a_k for k = 1, 2, ..., n. The letter k is a dummy index; the lower limit is 1 and the upper limit is n. So 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 = sum_{k=1}^{4} (2k-1).

PART 3 — Series vs sum.
The word 'series' refers to the indicated sum, not the resulting number. Writing 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 is the series; '16' is its value (sum). This distinction matters because an infinite series may or may not converge to a finite value.
keyMoves: Turn a sequence into its series by joining the terms with plus signs
Write the series compactly as the sum from k = 1 to n of a_k using sigma notation
Distinguish the series (the indicated sum) from its sum (the resulting number)
misconceptions: Confusing a series (the sum of terms) with the sequence (the list of terms)
Treating 'series' as the final number rather than the indicated sum

nodes.title: Recognise a geometric progression by a constant ratio
nodes.brief: Test whether a sequence is a GP by checking that each term (after the first) bears a constant non-zero ratio to the one before it.
nodes.quote: A sequence  $a_1, a_2, a_3, ..., a_n, ...$  is called *geometric progression*, if each term is non-zero and
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The definition of a G.P.
A sequence a_1, a_2, a_3, ... is a geometric progression (G.P.) if every term is non-zero and the ratio of each term to the term immediately before it is the same constant r, called the common ratio. Symbolically: a_{k+1}/a_k = r for every k >= 1.

PART 2 — Identifying a G.P. by inspection.
Divide each term by the one before it and check the quotients are all equal and non-zero. Example: 2, 4, 8, 16 gives ratios 4/2 = 2, 8/4 = 2, 16/8 = 2 — constant, so this is a G.P. with r = 2. For 1/9, -1/27, 1/81: ratios are (-1/27)/(1/9) = -1/3 and (1/81)/(-1/27) = -1/3 — G.P. with r = -1/3.

PART 3 — The key difference from an A.P.
An arithmetic progression (A.P.) has constant differences; a G.P. has constant ratios. Never add or subtract to check for a G.P. — divide. Also, a term of zero is not allowed in a G.P. because division by zero is undefined.
keyMoves: Divide each term by the term immediately before it
Confirm every such ratio is the same constant r and that no term is zero
Declare the sequence a G.P. exactly when that constant ratio holds throughout
misconceptions: Checking constant differences (an A.P. test) instead of constant ratios for a G.P.
Allowing a zero term, which is not permitted in a G.P.

nodes.title: Identify the first term a and common ratio r of a GP
nodes.brief: Pick out a (the first term) and r (the common ratio) so a GP can be written as a, ar, ar^2, ar^3, ....
nodes.quote: where a is called the *first term* and r is called the *common ratio* of the G.P.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Standard form of a G.P.
Letting a_1 = a, a G.P. can always be written as a, ar, ar^2, ar^3, ..., where a is the first term and r is the common ratio. Multiplying by r each time generates the next term.

PART 2 — Reading off a and r.
From a given G.P., a is simply the first term listed, and r = a_2 / a_1 (any consecutive pair works). Example: for 3, 6, 12, 24, a = 3 and r = 6/3 = 2, giving standard form 3, 3*2, 3*4, 3*8. Example: for 1/9, -1/27, 1/81, a = 1/9 and r = (-1/27)/(1/9) = -1/3.

PART 3 — Why this matters.
Every G.P. formula — the nth term a_n = ar^(n-1) and the sum S_n = a(r^n - 1)/(r-1) — requires knowing a and r. Identifying them correctly is the non-negotiable first step in any G.P. problem.
keyMoves: Read the first term a directly as a_1
Compute the common ratio r as a_2/a_1 (any term divided by the one before it)
Rewrite the G.P. in the standard form a, ar, ar^2, ar^3, ...
misconceptions: Taking r as the difference between terms rather than their ratio
Computing the ratio as a_1/a_2 (preceding over following) and inverting r

nodes.title: Find the nth term of a GP using a_n = ar^(n-1)
nodes.brief: Apply a_n = ar^(n-1) to compute a stated term of a GP, or to find which term equals a given value.
nodes.quote: the pattern suggests that the  $n^{\text{th}}$  term of a G.P. is given by  $a_n = ar^{n-1}$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Deriving the formula.
Listing G.P. terms: a_1 = a = ar^0, a_2 = ar = ar^1, a_3 = ar^2, ..., the exponent of r is always one less than the position. So the nth term is a_n = ar^(n-1). Example: for 5, 25, 125, ... (a = 5, r = 5) the 10th term is a_10 = 5 * 5^9 = 5^10 = 9765625.

PART 2 — Finding which term equals a given value.
Set ar^(n-1) equal to the target and solve for n by equating exponents. Example: which term of 2, 8, 32, ... equals 131072? Here a = 2, r = 4, so 2 * 4^(n-1) = 131072, giving 4^(n-1) = 65536 = 4^8, hence n-1 = 8 and n = 9.

PART 3 — Finding a and r from two given terms.
If a_3 = 24 and a_6 = 192 then dividing: a_6/a_3 = ar^5/(ar^2) = r^3 = 192/24 = 8, so r = 2. Then a = a_3/r^2 = 24/4 = 6. Always divide the later-term equation by the earlier to eliminate a.
keyMoves: Apply a_n = ar^(n-1) with the known first term a and common ratio r
To locate a given value, set ar^(n-1) equal to it and solve for n using equal powers
From two given terms, divide the equations a_m = ar^(m-1) to eliminate a and find r, then back-substitute
misconceptions: Using the exponent n instead of n-1 in ar^(n-1)
Confusing the G.P. nth term with the A.P. nth term a + (n-1)d

nodes.title: Sum the first n terms of a GP
nodes.brief: Use S_n = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1) for r != 1 (and S_n = na for r = 1) to add the first n terms of a GP.
nodes.quote: Let the first term of a G.P. be a and the common ratio be r. Let us denote by  $S_n$  the sum to first n terms of G.P.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Deriving the sum formula.
Write S_n = a + ar + ar^2 + ... + ar^(n-1). Multiply both sides by r: rS_n = ar + ar^2 + ... + ar^n. Subtract: S_n(1 - r) = a(1 - r^n), so S_n = a(1 - r^n)/(1 - r) = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1) for r != 1. When r = 1 every term equals a, so S_n = na.

PART 2 — Applying the formula (r != 1).
For the G.P. 3, 6, 12, 24, ... (a = 3, r = 2) the sum of the first 5 terms is S_5 = 3(2^5 - 1)/(2 - 1) = 3 * 31 / 1 = 93. Check: 3 + 6 + 12 + 24 + 48 = 93. Correct.

PART 3 — Solving for n from a given sum.
If S_n = 765 for the G.P. 3, 6, 12, ... then 3(2^n - 1)/(2 - 1) = 765, so 2^n - 1 = 255, 2^n = 256 = 2^8, n = 8. Verify: S_8 = 3*(256 - 1) = 765. Correct.
keyMoves: Identify a, r and n, then choose the r != 1 or r = 1 case
For r != 1 apply S_n = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1) (or a(1 - r^n)/(1 - r))
For r = 1 use S_n = na, and solve the formula for n when the target sum is given
misconceptions: Applying the r != 1 formula when r = 1 (dividing by zero)
Mixing up the numerator/denominator signs between a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1) and a(1 - r^n)/(1 - r)

nodes.title: Compute the geometric mean of two positive numbers
nodes.brief: Find the geometric mean of two positive numbers a and b as sqrt(ab), the middle term that makes a, G, b a GP.
nodes.quote: The geometric mean of two positive numbers a
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition of G.M.
The geometric mean (G.M.) of two positive numbers a and b is G = sqrt(ab). Example: G.M. of 2 and 8 is sqrt(16) = 4. G.M. of 3 and 27 is sqrt(81) = 9.

PART 2 — Why it forms a G.P.
The three numbers a, G, b are consecutive terms of a G.P. because G/a = sqrt(ab)/a = sqrt(b/a) and b/G = b/sqrt(ab) = sqrt(b/a) — the ratios are equal. For 2, 4, 8: ratio = 2 in both steps, confirming it is a G.P.

PART 3 — Inserting one geometric mean.
To find a single number to place between two given positive numbers p and q so that the three are in G.P., compute G = sqrt(pq). Example: between 5 and 45, G = sqrt(225) = 15; check: 5, 15, 45 has common ratio 3.
keyMoves: Compute the geometric mean of a and b as G = sqrt(ab)
Check that a, G, b then form a G.P. (equal ratios G/a = b/G)
Use the G.M. to interpolate the middle term of a three-term G.P.
misconceptions: Using the arithmetic mean (a + b)/2 in place of the geometric mean sqrt(ab)
Taking the geometric mean of numbers of opposite sign, where sqrt(ab) is not real

nodes.title: Insert n geometric means between two numbers
nodes.brief: Insert several numbers between a and b so the whole list is a GP, by treating b as the last term and solving for r.
nodes.quote: Given any two positive numbers a and b, we can insert as many numbers as we like between them to make the resulting sequence in a G.P.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Setting up the equation for r.
To insert n geometric means G_1, G_2, ..., G_n between two positive numbers a and b, the full list a, G_1, G_2, ..., G_n, b must be a G.P. with n+2 terms. Since b is the (n+2)th term, b = a * r^(n+1), so r = (b/a)^(1/(n+1)).

PART 2 — Computing the inserted means.
Once r is known, G_1 = ar, G_2 = ar^2, ..., G_n = ar^n. Example: insert 3 geometric means between 1 and 256. Here 256 = 1 * r^4, so r^4 = 256, r = 4. The means are G_1 = 4, G_2 = 16, G_3 = 64. Check: 1, 4, 16, 64, 256 — each ratio is 4. Correct.

PART 3 — Verification step.
Always verify by checking the ratio between consecutive terms in the full list. Also note: when taking the (n+1)th root, if a and b are both positive there is one positive real root for r (and a negative one if n is odd — take the positive root unless both endpoints are explicitly stated to be of opposite sign).
keyMoves: Set b as the (n+2)th term: b = a r^(n+1), and solve r = (b/a)^(1/(n+1))
Compute the inserted means as G_1 = ar, G_2 = ar^2, ..., G_n = ar^n
Check the full list a, G_1, ..., G_n, b is a G.P. with the found r
misconceptions: Treating b as the (n+1)th rather than the (n+2)th term when n means are inserted
Forgetting the negative root of r when only positive interpolation is intended (or vice versa)

nodes.title: Relate the AM and GM of two positive numbers (A >= G)
nodes.brief: Use A = (a+b)/2 and G = sqrt(ab) with the inequality A >= G to find or compare two positive numbers.
nodes.quote: From (1), we obtain the relationship  $A \ge G$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definitions and the inequality.
For two positive numbers a and b, the arithmetic mean is A = (a + b)/2 and the geometric mean is G = sqrt(ab). Their difference is A - G = (a + b)/2 - sqrt(ab) = (sqrt(a) - sqrt(b))^2 / 2, which is always >= 0. Therefore A >= G, with equality if and only if a = b.

PART 2 — Using A >= G to reconstruct two numbers.
If A.M. = 10 and G.M. = 8 then a + b = 20 and ab = 64. Now (a - b)^2 = (a + b)^2 - 4ab = 400 - 256 = 144, so a - b = 12 (taking positive root since a >= b). Solving a + b = 20 and a - b = 12 gives a = 16, b = 4. Check: (16 + 4)/2 = 10 and sqrt(64) = 8. Correct.

PART 3 — Applications of A >= G.
The inequality A >= G provides a lower bound for sums: for positive x, x + 1/x >= 2 (set a = x, b = 1/x, so A = (x + 1/x)/2 >= sqrt(x * 1/x) = 1, giving x + 1/x >= 2). Equality holds at x = 1.
keyMoves: Write A = (a + b)/2 and G = sqrt(ab) for the two positive numbers
Use A - G = (sqrt(a) - sqrt(b))^2 / 2 >= 0 to justify A >= G
From given A and G, solve a + b = 2A and ab = G^2 (via (a-b)^2 = (a+b)^2 - 4ab) for the numbers
misconceptions: Believing G can exceed A for positive numbers (the inequality is always A >= G)
Forgetting the equality case A = G occurs only when a = b

nodes.title: Solve sequence and GP problems using terms, sums and means
nodes.brief: Combine the nth-term and sum formulas with the geometric mean and the AM-GM relation to solve any GP-based sequence-and-series problem.
nodes.quote: every term except the first term bears a constant ratio to the term immediately preceding it.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The G.P. toolkit.
A G.P. is identified by its first term a and common ratio r; from these, the nth term is a_n = ar^(n-1) and the sum of n terms is S_n = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1) for r != 1 (or na for r = 1). The geometric mean G = sqrt(ab) gives the middle term of a 3-term G.P., and the inequality A >= G relates the arithmetic and geometric means.

PART 2 — Problem-solving strategy.
Step 1: identify a and r (directly from the sequence, or by solving from two given terms or a given sum). Step 2: choose the right formula. Step 3: solve and interpret. Example: a G.P. has first term 3 and sum of first 4 terms 45. Then 3(r^4 - 1)/(r - 1) = 45, so (r^4 - 1)/(r - 1) = 15, hence r^3 + r^2 + r + 1 = 15, giving r = 2 by trial (2^3 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 15). The 4 terms are 3, 6, 12, 24.

PART 3 — Common contexts.
Geometric growth appears in compound interest (balance multiplies by (1 + p/100) each year), depreciation (value multiplies by (1 - p/100) each year), and doubling (bacteria, ancestors). Recognise these as G.P. contexts, pick a and r, then apply the nth-term or sum formula.
keyMoves: Identify a and r (directly, or by solving from given terms/sums)
Select and apply the right tool: a_n = ar^(n-1), S_n = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1), G.M. = sqrt(ab), or A >= G
Interpret the answer in the problem's context (growth, interest, depreciation, interpolation)
misconceptions: Reaching for a new formula instead of reducing a problem to the nth-term or sum formula
Treating a geometric situation (doubling, fixed-percentage change) as arithmetic
